notes:
Live codex-cli 0.144.5 PROBE (META-HARNESS-122, probe 2 / dialogBackoutKeys): which ESC encoding actually dismisses the /permissions dialog when written by a synthetic PTY writer (not a real terminal, which would do its own kitty-protocol encoding of the ESC key).

Recorded by test/corpus/tools/probe-codex-permissions.ts --phase backout --open-shape split, under an isolated CODEX_HOME seeded per test/helpers/codex_home.ts (auth.json only, no config.toml). Boot rendered the folder-trust interstitial (a bare '\r' did NOT dismiss it; '\x1b[13u' did on the next rung of the ladder), confirming the same is-this-a-real-Enter question applies beyond the three parseMenuOptions families.

RESULT: BOTH candidates dismissed the /permissions dialog cleanly in this same session — bare '\x1b' (dialog_dismissed=true) and CSI 27 u '\x1b[27u' (dialog_dismissed=true). Neither left any residue in the composer afterward (composer_row read back to the placeholder-backed idle row both times). dialogBackoutKeys() can therefore be a bare ESC: '\x1b' (single byte 0x1b) — the simpler encoding, and the one a human hitting the physical Esc key would send.

NON-OVERLAP NOTE for the fake-harness subtask: a bare ESC ('\x1b', one byte, 0x1b) is a byte-for-byte PREFIX of CSI 13 u ('\x1b[13u', ready.ts:312 / codex.ts:240) and of CSI 27 u ('\x1b[27u'). A matcher that tests 'first byte is 0x1b' is not sufficient to distinguish an ESC key from a CSI-u sequence; it must check that 0x1b is EITHER the entire write OR is not immediately followed by '[' — equivalently, match the exact byte string '\x1b' (length 1) and treat any '\x1b[...' as a distinct, non-ESC sequence.

CONTAINMENT: real ~/.codex/config.toml and ~/.codex/auth.json were both sha256-identical before and after (see containment block). stdin.log records every keystroke escape-encoded and IS the byte pin for this session.
